programs.name: ARKansas Medicaid

eligibility.income_limits: For long-term care programs like Nursing Home Medicaid (single applicant in 2026): under $2,982/month. ARSeniors: at or below 80% FPL (assets under $9,430 individual/$14,130 couple). SSI-Related: under SSI limits (federal benefits rate). Limits vary by program, marital status, and household; spousal allowances and Miller Trusts available for excess income. General Medicaid for elderly/disabled has income/asset tests; expansion up to 138% FPL for non-elderly adults but not primary for seniors[1][5][7].
eligibility.asset_limits: Typically $2,000 for individual (Nursing Home, SSI-Related); $3,000 for eligible couple (SSI-Related). ARSeniors: $9,430 individual/$14,130 couple. Countable assets include cash, bank accounts, investments, second vehicles/homes (primary residence, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots exempt). 5-year look-back period for transfers/gifts may impose penalties[1][3][6][7].
other_requirements: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) for Nursing Home Medicaid and waivers; functional need for ADLs for regular Medicaid LTC[1]
Arkansas residency proof[2]
Medical/functional need for long-term care (e.g., nursing home, HCBS, assisted living)[1][2]
For specific benefits like home modifications: inability to live safely independently[1]

benefits.value: Long-term care services including nursing home care (unlimited duration if qualified), home/community-based services (HCBS) like in-home personal care, adult foster care, assisted living (full Medicaid benefits but room/board not covered in Level II; requires Intermediate Level of Care), non-medical supports for frail seniors at home. Covers healthcare not provided by Medicare (e.g., personal care, LTSS)[1][3][6][9].

methods: Online: Access Arkansas (https://access.arkansas.gov)
In-person: Local Department of Human Services (DHS) county office
Mail: To local DHS office
application.processing_time: Coverage can begin 3 months prior to approval; applications may take longer than expected—ask for usual timeframe upon submission[3]
application.waitlist: Not specified in sources; may vary by service demand[3]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; apply via Access Arkansas or DHS
documents: Proof of income (wages, Social Security, pensions)
Proof of assets
Proof of Arkansas residency
Medical/functional assessments for NFLOC
Financial history for 5-year look-back

geography.restrictions: None statewide; available to AR residents
geography.regional_variations: Administered via local DHS county offices; potential differences in processing/wait times or providers by county/region not detailed[5][6]

programs.official_source: https://humanservices.arkansas.gov (Arkansas Department of Human Services); apply at https://access.arkansas.gov[5][6]
gotchas: Multiple programs with varying income/asset limits (e.g., $2,000 vs. ARSeniors $9,430)—must select correct one[1][7]
5-year look-back penalizes asset transfers/gifts[6]
Medicare does not cover long-term care; Medicaid fills gap but requires NFLOC[1][6]
Spousal impoverishment rules apply (resource division but limited income sharing)[9]
Room/board not covered in some assisted living[9]
Spend-down or Miller Trusts needed if over limits[2][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Multiple tiers/programs (Nursing Home, HCBS waivers, ARSeniors, SSI-Related) with differing income/asset thresholds and care levels (NFLOC vs. Intermediate); annual changes, spousal rules, 5-year look-back unique to LTC Medicaid

programs.name: ARChoices in Homecare

eligibility.age: 21-64 with physical disability or 65 and older[1][2][6]
eligibility.income_limits: Determined by DHS Division of County Operations as Medicaid financial eligibility; specific dollar amounts or household size table not detailed in sources, follows standard Medicaid income criteria with strategies available to qualify if initially over limits[3][4][7]
eligibility.asset_limits: Medicaid resource limits apply; home equity interest no greater than $752,000 in 2026 (home value minus mortgage); home exempt if applicant lives there or has intent to return, or if spouse/minor child/permanently disabled child lives there[2]
other_requirements: Arkansas resident[1]
Require nursing home level of care (intermediate for 21-64, skilled may disqualify)[2][4]

Functional need: unable to perform at least 1 ADL (eating, toileting, transferring/locomotion) without substantial assistance/dependence OR at least 2 ADLs without limited assistance OR primary/secondary diagnosis of Alzheimer's/related dementia with cognitive impairment requiring substantial supervision; assessed via ARIA tool assigning Tier 2 (eligible), Tiers 0/1 too low, Tier 3 too high; final determination by DHS Office of Long Term Care nurse[2][3][4]
Physically disabled (21-64) or frail elderly (65+); diagnosis of severe mental illness/ID does not bar if other criteria met[1][4][5]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services including assistance with everyday activities (bathing, dressing, mobility in home, meal prep, household chores); specific services to meet nursing home level of care needs; person-centered service plan and individual services budget established post-assessment; self-direction option via Independent Choices[1][2][6]

methods: In-person or mail at DHS office in county of residence[3][5]
Start with Department of County Operations (DCO) for financial eligibility; phone Access Arkansas at 855-372-1084[6]
Referral process: DCO to DAABHS, then ARIA assessment by Optum RN, Level of Care by OLTC, DAABHS RN home visit for service plan[3]
application.processing_time: Eligibility begins date DHS Division of County Operations approves unless provisional plan of care; no specific timeline stated[5]
forms: No specific form names/numbers listed; apply via DHS county office and ARIA assessment system[3]
documents: Not explicitly listed; prepare for financial eligibility (income/resources proof), medical documentation for functional needs/level of care, licensed medical professional determination[3][4]

geography.regional_variations: Apply at county DHS office; potential regional wait times/providers not detailed[3][5]
offices_or_providers: DHS county offices statewide; providers must enroll per Medicaid manual[3]

gotchas: Must meet exact Tier 2 in ARIA assessment (Tier 0/1 too low needs, Tier 3 too high for waiver)[2]
Financial eligibility via Medicaid rules; spend-down strategies needed if over limits[7]
Nursing home level of care required; skilled care may disqualify[4]
Apply first at local DHS county office, not directly to waiver[3][5]
Home equity limit $752,000 in 2026; exemptions narrow[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered functional assessment (0-3) determines eligibility; Medicaid financial criteria without specified income table; county-based application with centralized assessment; no fixed hours/dollars, individualized budget

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No strict income limits for program eligibility; follows Medicaid long-term care rules for coverage without premium. Income under 300% of Federal Benefit Rate ($2,901/month in 2025) for Medicaid eligibility, but applicants over limit may qualify via Income Trust. Spousal impoverishment protections apply for community spouses.[4][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: For Medicaid coverage: $2,000 or less for applicant (excluding primary home). No asset test for private pay participants who meet medical criteria.[4][5][6]
other_requirements: Certified by Arkansas Office of Long Term Care to need nursing facility level of care (one of four levels: Skilled-S, Intermediate I-A, II-B, III-C).
Live in service area of a PACE provider in Arkansas.
Able to live safely in community (home or assisted living) with PACE support.
U.S. citizen or qualified alien.
Arkansas resident.
Social Security number required.
Under 65 must prove disability via SSI/SSA or Medical Review Team.
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, hospice, or certain other programs.

benefits.value: All Medicare/Medicaid-covered services (primary, preventive, acute, long-term care) plus additional medically necessary services approved by interdisciplinary team: doctor visits, prescription drugs, transportation, home care, personal care, day center services, hospitalization, therapy, dental, vision, memory care support. No deductibles/co-pays. Provided at PACE centers, home, inpatient facilities. Personalized care plan by healthcare team.[1][5]

methods: Contact Arkansas Department of Human Services (DHS) county office or PACE provider (e.g., PACE of the Ozarks).
Initial medical assessment via DHS Registered Nurse (RN), then financial eligibility at local DHS county office.
Provider-specific: PACE of the Ozarks (Westfork/Winslow areas). No specific URL, phone, or form listed in sources; start with DHS.
Private pay option if non-medical criteria not met but medical criteria are.
application.waitlist: Not specified; may vary by provider/service area.
forms: No specific form names/numbers provided; DHS handles financial eligibility application.
documents: Proof of residency, citizenship/qualified alien status.
Social Security number.
Income and asset documentation for Medicaid financial eligibility.
Medical records for nursing facility level of care assessment.

geography.restrictions: Limited to areas served by PACE providers (e.g., PACE of the Ozarks serves Westfork 72774, Winslow 72959, and surrounding Ozarks region). Not available statewide.
geography.regional_variations: Services via specific providers; eligibility/assessment uniform but availability and wait times differ by service delivery area.
offices_or_providers: PACE of the Ozarks (Ozarks region, specific ZIPs: Westfork 72774, Winslow 72959). Local DHS county offices for applications.

gotchas: Not statewide—must live in limited service areas (e.g., only Ozarks region via one main provider).
No Medicare/Medicaid enrollment required to join, but most are dual-eligible; private pay possible for non-Medicaid.
Temporary medical conditions (<21 days) don't disqualify.
Cannot be in Medicare Advantage, hospice, or certain other plans.
Medicaid income over limit? Use Income Trust—ask DHS caseworker.
Medical eligibility via state Office of Long Term Care, separate from financial.
programs.data_shape_notes: Limited to specific regions/providers (not statewide); no financial eligibility barrier but Medicaid rules for full coverage; private pay option; nursing home-level care certification required.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits: Income limits vary by program and household size (2026 figures; include $20 general income disregard). QMB: Individual $1,235-$1,350/month, Married $1,663-$1,824/month (≤100% FPL). SLMB: Individual $1,478-$1,526/month, Married $1,992-$2,064/month (>100%-≤120% FPL). QI: Individual $1,660-$1,715/month, Married $2,239-$2,320/month (>120%-≤135% FPL). ARSeniors (full Medicaid): Lower thresholds, aged 65+.[3][4][5][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Federal limits apply: $9,090-$9,950 single, $13,630-$14,910 married/couple. Counts: Bank accounts, stocks, bonds. Exempt: Home, one car, burial plots, life insurance (up to $1,500 face value), personal belongings.[3][5][6]
other_requirements: Must be entitled/eligible for Medicare Part A (QMB/SLMB/QI; ARSeniors exempt but cannot opt out of Medicare if eligible)
Aged, blind, or disabled (except ARSeniors: aged 65+ only)
U.S. citizen or qualified alien
Arkansas resident
Not eligible for QI if in other Medicaid category simultaneously (must choose)

benefits.value: QMB: Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums/deductible/coinsurance/copays. SLMB/QI: Part B premiums only ($202.90/month in 2026). ARSeniors: Full Medicaid benefits + QMB coverage.

methods: Online: www.access.arkansas.gov
Mail or in-person: Local county DHS office
application.processing_time: QMB: Up to 45 days from complete info (effective first of next month). SLMB/QI: Up to 45 days, retroactive up to 3 months prior (QI not before Jan 1 of application year). ARSeniors: Retroactive 3 months.
application.waitlist: QI limited funding, first-come first-served (federal cap).
forms: DHS-3227 (Medicaid Application); request via phone/local office
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI/SS awards)
Proof of resources/assets (bank statements)
Medicare card/Part A entitlement proof
ID (birth certificate, Social Security card)
Proof of residency
Alien status if non-citizen

geography.regional_variations: Processing via local county DHS offices; possible wait time differences by county workload.

gotchas: QI has limited federal funding (first-come, first-served; may run out)
Must choose QI over other Medicaid/spend-down (no simultaneous coverage)
Income/resources counted strictly; $20 disregard applies but verify current FPL adjustments
QMB no retroactive coverage; apply early
Arkansas uses federal asset limits (some states eliminated); home/car exempt but list all
Entitled to Medicare but not enrolled? May still qualify if eligible
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by income brackets (QMB <100% FPL, SLMB 100-120%, QI 120-135%); ARSeniors as lowest tier with full Medicaid; QI funding-capped and non-retroactive before Jan 1; county DHS processing with statewide uniformity but local offices.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)

eligibility.income_limits: For households with a member 60 or older or disabled (Oct 1, 2025 - Sept 30, 2026): No gross income limit. Net income must be at or below 100% of federal poverty guidelines. Gross income limits (130% FPL) apply only to households without elderly/disabled: 1 person $1695/month, 2 $2291, 3 $2887, 4 $3482, 5 $4079, 6 $4674, 7 $5270, +$595 each additional. For seniors: e.g., $15,060/year ($1,255/month) for 1 person, $20,440/year ($1,703/month) for 2 people.[1][2][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: Households with member 60+ or disabled: $4,500 countable resources. All households: $3,000 (or $2,250 in some charts). Temporary 1-year increase to $5,500 available (once every 5 years). Countable: bank accounts, savings certificates, non-exempt vehicles (value over $4,650 per employed/student member). Exempt: home, one household vehicle, income-producing vehicles/property, life insurance, burial spaces, household goods.[1][3][4]
other_requirements: U.S. citizenship or legal alien status
Residency in Arkansas
Social Security Number
Net income calculation includes deductions for medical expenses (over $35/month for elderly/disabled), shelter, etc.
Work requirement exempt for 60+ (applies to able-bodied adults 18-54 without dependents: 20 hours/week)
Categorically eligible if all members receive SSI or household receives TEA (no income/resource test)

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card benefits for food purchases (amount based on household size, income, deductions; e.g., max allotment for 1-person elderly/disabled household calculated as max minus 30% net income). Restaurant Meals Program available for eligible elderly/disabled.[1][5][7]

methods: Online: https://www.accessarkansas.org
In-person: Local DHS county offices
Mail: Download form from humanservices.arkansas.gov and mail to local office
application.processing_time: Up to 30 days standard; expedited within 7 days if very low income/no assets.[1]
forms: SNAP Application (available on Access Arkansas or DHS site)
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, Social Security award letters)
Proof of expenses (rent/mortgage, utilities, medical bills over $35/month)
ID (driver's license, birth certificate)
Social Security Numbers for all household members
Bank statements for assets
Proof of disability if claiming (doctor's note, SSI award)

geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly by Arkansas DHS county offices; no major benefit variations, but local office processing may vary slightly.[1][3]
offices_or_providers: Local DHS County Offices statewide (find via humanservices.arkansas.gov/locations)

gotchas: No gross income test for elderly/disabled households—many miss this and think they earn too much.[1]
High medical expenses (> $35/month) deductible for 60+, boosting net income eligibility.[2][5]
Assets include most savings but exempt home/primary vehicle; temporary $5,500 limit often overlooked.[3]
Must include all who buy/prepare food together in household size.[2]
Social Security/VA/disability counts as income.[2]
EBT only for eligible food; Restaurant Meals Program for elderly/disabled in AR.[7]
programs.data_shape_notes: No gross income limit for households with elderly (60+) or disabled; higher asset limit ($4,500 vs $3,000); benefits/allotments scale by household size and net income deductions (medical key for seniors); statewide via DHS with county offices.

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly income must meet Federal Income Guidelines at or below 60% of state median income (SMI), varying by household size. Most recent chart (2025): 1: $2,251; 2: $2,944; 3: $3,636; 4: $4,329; add $672 per additional member. Other sources list slightly varying figures like 1: $2,347; 2: $3,069; 3: $3,791; 4: $4,514; 5: $5,236; 6: $5,958 (2025) or regional 1: $1,936; 2: $2,531; 3: $3,127; 4: $3,723; 5: $4,318. Based on previous month's countable income.[1][2][6][7][8]
eligibility.asset_limits: Must meet countable resource standard, including cash, checking/savings accounts, CDs, cryptocurrency, stocks, bonds. Specific limits not detailed; exemptions not specified.[1]
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or legal resident non-citizen.
Responsible for home energy bill (not paid by non-resident).
Household defined as all under one roof/one utility meter; all must apply together.
Residential only; no businesses.
If utilities in rent, provide lease showing energy burden (different for subsidized/unsubsidized housing).

benefits.value: Regular: Fixed amount via approved model based on income, household size, energy source (electricity, gas, propane, wood, pellets); max heating $475, min $50; max cooling $287, min $50. Crisis: Up to $500 (summer/winter) for shut-off, disconnection, depleted fuel, or life-threatening situations. Payments direct to vendor. One regular and one crisis per household per season. Benefit matrix from Arkansas Energy Office.[1][2]

methods: Contact Community-Based Organization (CBO) serving your county for instructions (in-person, phone, mail).
Download LIHEAP Application Form from local CBO sites (e.g., cscdc.net, crdcnea.tech, eoawc.org).
Example application via ADEQ.
Call local agency; no statewide phone listed—find via ADEQ or local CBO.
application.processing_time: Varies; longer during high volume periods. No specific statewide timeline.[7]
application.waitlist: Funding limited; applications may close early if funds exhausted. No formal waitlist mentioned.[2]
forms: LIHEAP Application (standard form, downloadable from CBOs like CSCDC, CRDCNEA, EOA).
Abbreviated form for some SNAP recipients.
documents: Photo ID.
Social Security card/SSN for household members 18+; SSNs for minors.
Most recent utility bills (proof of residency/energy cost).
Proof of income (previous month, all adult household members).
Proof of SSN.
Lease if utilities included in rent.
Contribution statement if non-relative assists financially.

geography.restrictions: Apply via CBO serving your county; serves all Arkansas counties.
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local CBOs with potential differences in income charts, processing, offices, wait times. E.g., EOA serves only Washington County; CRDCNEA eight-county area. Benefit matrix statewide but local application.[1][3][6][7]
offices_or_providers: Community-Based Organizations (CBOs) by county; find via ADEQ or local sites like CSCDC, EOA, CRDCNEA.

gotchas: Household = all under one roof/one meter; roommates count even if not sharing expenses.
Only 1 regular + 1 crisis per season per household; no duplicates.
Must be responsible for bill—not eligible if paid by non-resident.
Seasonal: Heating Jan-Mar, Cooling Jul-Aug, Crisis Jan-Apr/Jul-Sep; funds run out early.
Income based on prior month; varying charts by source/CBO—verify locally.
Elderly priority not explicitly stated; no age requirement but crisis for threats.
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered via county CBOs with local variations in charts/forms; benefits via state matrix scaled by income/size/fuel; seasonal with early fund exhaustion; household strictly by utility meter.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 200% of federal poverty guidelines. Example guidelines (likely from prior year, check current via provider): 1: $23,760; 2: $32,040; 3: $40,320; 4: $48,600; 5: $56,880; 6: $65,160; 7: $73,460; 8: $81,780. Varies by household size and updated annually per OMB/DOE guidelines in WPN notices[1][2][3][4].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in sources[1][2][3][4].
other_requirements: Categorical eligibility if household has SSI recipient[1][2][3].
LIHEAP eligibility may qualify (up to 60% Arkansas median income)[2].
Eligible housing: owner-occupied or rental (landlord agreement for renters); non-stationary trailers must have axles removed, residential utilities, street address mail (no PO Box)[2].
Priority for elderly (60+), disabled (e.g., SSI/SSDI/Vet disability/Rehab Services), children under 7, Native Americans[1][2][3].
Must meet building eligibility (energy inefficient home)[2][4].

benefits.value: Free services: energy audit/inspection by DOE-trained auditor, weatherization measures (attic/wall insulation, weather-stripping doors/windows, caulking/sealing cracks, storm windows, furnace retrofitting), health/safety upgrades, energy-efficient heating equipment. No cost to household; done by crews/contractors[1][3][4][6].

methods: Contact local Community Action Agency (CAA) via https://www.acaaa.org/ to find provider by county[1].
Provider examples: BCD (bcdinc.org), CSCDC (cscdc.net), CADC (cadc.com)[3][5][6].
In-person/mail/online forms via local provider (e.g., CSCDC 2025 Application requires 12 months utility bills)[9].
application.processing_time: Not specified; after eligibility, local agency schedules energy inspection, then work order and completion. Operational year-round[1][4].
application.waitlist: Due to limited funding, priority points used; re-certify eligibility every 12 months until served[1][2].
forms: Local CAA application with household income proof; Tenant Rights and Lessor Agreement for renters[3].
CSCDC Weatherization Application (requires 12 months gas/propane/electric bills)[9].
documents: Proof of all household income (payroll stubs/checks, employer/Employment Office/SSA statements, assistance/support statements); unemployed: Employment Office statement[3][9].
12 months utility bills (gas, propane, electric as applicable)[9].
For renters: Landlord Lessor Agreement, Tenant Rights form[3].

geography.restrictions: All 75 Arkansas counties; single-family homes, apartments, manufactured homes if eligible[4][7].
geography.regional_variations: Administered by 5 service providers (CAAs/nonprofits) covering regions; apply to local CAA by county; no specific wait time differences noted[1][4].
offices_or_providers: Network of Community Action Agencies via https://www.acaaa.org/[1].
Examples: Better Community Development (BCD), Crawford-Sebastian CDC (CSCDC), Community Action Agencies of Arkansas (CADC)[3][5][6].

gotchas: Must apply through specific local CAA by county, not statewide office[1].
Renters need landlord Lessor Agreement[3].
Trailers/campers: strict rules (no axles, residential utilities, street address)[2].
Priority-based due to funding limits; SSI auto-eligible but others compete[1][2][3].
Re-certify income every 12 months until served[2].
Home must pass energy/health/safety inspection and building eligibility[1][2][4].
Income guidelines update annually; example figures may be outdated—verify current[3].
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide via 5 regional CAA/nonprofit providers with priority tiers; income at 200% poverty or categorical (SSI/LIHEAP); no asset test; building eligibility required alongside client eligibility.

programs.name: Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries, families, and caregivers[1][2][7]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial tests apply[1][2]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver in Arkansas; no other restrictions[1][2][7]

benefits.value: Outreach and education on Medicare fraud prevention; one-on-one counseling; advocacy to resolve billing disputes; referrals for suspected fraud, errors, or abuse; community presentations and materials; often co-located with SHIP health insurance counseling[1][2][4]

methods: Email: [email protected]
Website: https://insurance.arkansas.gov/consumer-assistance/senior-medicare-patrol-smp/
Schedule free speaker or report issues via phone/email[2]
application.processing_time: Immediate assistance for counseling and referrals; no formal application processing[1][2][4]
forms: No specific forms required for receiving services; volunteer applications available via Arkansas Insurance Department website[2][3]
documents: Medicare Summary Notice (MSN) or billing statements if reporting specific issues; no documents needed for general education/outreach[1][6]

geography.restrictions: None; available to all Arkansas Medicare beneficiaries[2]
geography.regional_variations: Administered statewide by Arkansas Insurance Department; no regional differences noted[2]
offices_or_providers: Arkansas Insurance Department (statewide contact); local SMP volunteers conduct services[2]

gotchas: Not a financial assistance or healthcare benefits program—focuses solely on fraud prevention, education, and advocacy; often confused with benefit-paying programs; volunteers handle services, so availability depends on local staffing; many sites also offer SHIP counseling, which is separate[1][2][4]
No monetary benefits or direct payments; people miss that it's preventive education, not eligibility-based aid[1][7]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income test; volunteer-based education and advocacy program; not a benefits entitlement; statewide with phone/email access; co-administered with SHIP in Arkansas[1][2][4]

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Arkansas)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for Older Americans Act Nutrition Program funding (approximately 39% of program funding). Income limits may apply only to meals funded through Social Services Block Grant (SSBG) sources, which vary by area agency on aging.[2][7]
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available sources. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for SSBG-funded program details if applicable.[7]
other_requirements: Must be homebound or unable to leave home due to disability, illness, or frailty[1][8]
Unable to prepare meals for themselves[1][2][8]
No caregiver in the home to help with meal preparation[1]
Must live within the meal delivery service area[1][3]

Exceptions: Disabled individuals under 60 may qualify if homebound and unable to prepare meals; spouse of eligible 60+ participant may qualify; disabled dependents living with eligible participant may qualify[1][4][7]

benefits.value: Home-delivered meals. Specific frequency (daily, weekly, number of meals per week) not detailed in search results. All menus approved by registered dietician.[8]

methods: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging (primary method)[3][5]
Visit local senior center[2]
In-person at Area Agency on Aging office[3]
Phone contact with Area Agency on Aging[5]
application.processing_time: Varies by program. Some process applications within one week; others take longer if there is a waiting list.[3]
application.waitlist: Waiting lists may exist depending on local program capacity.[3]
application.forms: Application form (specific form name/number not provided in sources). Some programs may require referral letter from doctor or social worker.[5]
documents: Proof of age[3]
Proof of residence[3]
Medical information[3]
Dietary restrictions (food allergies, diabetes)[2]
Emergency contacts[2]
Current medical issues (including chronic conditions)[2]
Prescription list[2]
Adaptive equipment used (walker, lift chair, etc.)[2]
Primary care physician contact information[2]
Secondary or specialist physician contact information[2]
Health insurance information[2]
General timeline of any hospitalizations[2]
Financial documents (only if program uses means test, such as SSBG-funded programs)[3]

geography.restrictions: Must live within designated delivery zone of local Area Agency on Aging. Each local program has its own geographic coverage.[3]
geography.regional_variations: Eligibility requirements and age/mobility exceptions vary by area and provider.[4][6] Processing times vary by region.[3] Some programs may serve temporarily ill or homebound seniors recovering from surgery; availability varies by region.[5]
geography.offices_or_providers: Program implemented through local chapters of Arkansas Association of Area Agencies on Aging. Specific office locations vary by region and must be verified with your local chapter.[2]

programs.official_source: https://agingarkansas.org/ (Arkansas Association of Area Agencies on Aging)
gotchas: Income is NOT a barrier to eligibility for most Meals on Wheels funding (Older Americans Act Nutrition Program). This is a common misconception.[2]
Eligibility varies significantly by region and local provider—requirements that apply in one county may not apply in another.[4][6]
Disabled individuals under 60 CAN qualify if homebound and unable to prepare meals, but this exception varies by area.[4][7]
If an applicant can attend a local senior center or food pantry, they may be directed to congregate meals instead of home delivery.[2]
Some programs require a doctor or social worker referral letter—check with your local program first.[5]
Spouses of eligible participants may qualify even if they don't meet age requirements, but this is determined on a case-by-case basis by the area agency on aging.[1][7]
Waiting lists exist in some areas; processing time is not guaranteed to be quick.[3]
You must live within the service delivery area—seniors outside service areas must seek alternative programs.[3]
The program prioritizes those with greatest economic or social needs, which may increase nutritional risk.[7]
programs.data_shape_notes: This program is locally administered with no statewide uniform eligibility criteria or benefit structure. ## Critical Notes for Families

**Start here:** Contact your local **Area Agency on Aging** directly.[3][5] This is the only way to get accurate, region-specific information for your situation. The program is decentralized, and what applies in one part of Arkansas may not apply in another.[4][6]

**Income is not a barrier:** Unlike many assistance programs, Meals on Wheels does not use income as a primary eligibility factor for most funding sources.[2] The program focuses on functional need (inability to prepare meals, homebound status) rather than financial status.

**Exceptions matter:** If your loved one is under 60 but disabled and homebound, they may still qualify—but this varies by region.[4][7] Similarly, spouses of eligible participants may qualify even if they don't meet standard requirements.[1][7]

**Specific details are missing:** The search results do not provide the actual phone numbers, website URLs, or specific form names for Arkansas Area Agencies on Aging. You will need to search for your local agency by county or region to get contact information and application details.

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program

eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned; not applicable.[1]
other_requirements: Care recipient must be at least 60 years old (no age limit if they have Alzheimer's or related dementia).[1]

Caregiver must assist with activities such as transportation, housework, meal preparation, medication reminders, medical appointments, managing finances, dressing, or managing chronic conditions like diabetes or dementia.[1]
Contact local Area Agency on Aging for grant-specific requirements, which may include diagnosis, age, or location.[3]

benefits.value: Respite grants, counseling, training, and access to assistance services including respite care; specific services and amounts vary by grant (e.g., home care, day care, or facility care, but not duplicating other funding sources).[1][3][5]

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (no specific statewide phone or URL listed; visit agingarkansas.org for details).[1][3]
For specific grants like CareLink: Apply through Alzheimer's Arkansas Programs and Services, 201 Markham Center Drive, Little Rock AR 72205 (regional).[5]
forms: Varies by grant; e.g., 4-Step Application Process for CareLink grant (no form number).[5]
documents: Physician certification of chronic illness for some grants (e.g., CareLink); proof care recipient not receiving duplicate services from insurance, Medicaid, etc.[5]

geography.restrictions: Administered locally; some grants county-specific (e.g., CareLink limited to Pulaski, Saline, Monroe, Prairie, Lonoke, Faulkner counties).[5]
geography.regional_variations: Eligibility, services, and providers vary by local Area Agency on Aging and specific grants; contact local agency for details.[1][3][5]
offices_or_providers: Local Area Agencies on Aging; Alzheimer's Arkansas for central AR grants (Pulaski etc.).[1][3][5]

gotchas: Not all grants are identical; each has unique requirements like specific diagnosis or county residency—always check with local Area Agency on Aging.[3][5]
Services cannot duplicate other funding (e.g., Medicaid, Medicare, private insurance).[5]
This is distinct from VA or Medicaid paid caregiver programs.[2][4]
Primarily respite grants, not ongoing paid caregiving.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by local Area Agencies on Aging and grants; no statewide income/asset test; services vary by grant and region (e.g., county-restricted like CareLink); no fixed hours/dollars listed.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 125% of the current Health and Human Services Poverty Guidelines (federal poverty level). Exact dollar amounts vary annually and by household size; families must check current HHS Poverty Guidelines for precise figures, as no specific table provided in Arkansas sources[1][4].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program documentation[1][4].
other_requirements: Resident of the State of Arkansas[1][4]
Not currently employed at the time of application[1][4]
Low employment prospects (inherent to program design)[1]

National priority for veterans, qualified spouses, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, low literacy, limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless/at risk, or prior American Job Center users[2]

benefits.value: Part-time paid work (average 20 hours/week) at the higher of federal, state, or local minimum wage; on-the-job training at non-profit or public agencies (e.g., schools, hospitals, senior centers); job search skills development; assistance from Employment and Training Coordinator to find permanent unsubsidized part-time/full-time jobs[1][2][3][4].

methods: Email: [email protected][1]
Mail: P.O. Box 1437, Slot S530, Little Rock, Arkansas 72203-1437[1]
In-person: Arkansas Department of Human Services, 700 Main Street, 5th Floor, Little Rock, Arkansas 72201[1]
Contact Division of Aging, Adult, and Behavioral Health Services (Gary Hinkle) for local intake[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified; enrollment if eligible and no waitlist[5].
application.waitlist: Possible waitlist if demand exceeds slots; check with local office[5].
forms: No specific form name/number listed; complete application at local SCSEP office or via contact[1][4][5]
documents: Proof of age (e.g., ID showing 55+)[1][4]
Proof of Arkansas residency[1][4]
Proof of unemployment status[1][4]
Income documentation to verify ≤125% federal poverty level[1][4]

geography.regional_variations: Administered statewide by Arkansas Department of Human Services, Division of Aging, Adult, and Behavioral Health Services; placements at local community/government agencies may vary by availability, but no explicit regional differences in rules, wait times, or providers noted[1].
offices_or_providers: Primary: Little Rock office (700 Main Street, 5th Floor, Little Rock, AR 72201)[1]

gotchas: Must be unemployed at application; cannot be currently working[1][4]
Income limit is 125% of federal poverty guidelines—check current annual figures as they adjust and scale by household size[1][2]
Priority enrollment for veterans, over-65s, disabled, etc., may delay others[2]

programs.name: Legal Aid of Arkansas (Senior Legal Services)

eligibility.income_limits:
Maximum income is 125% of Federal Poverty Guidelines (National Eligibility Level) for standard eligibility; up to 200% (Maximum Income Level) if specific factors allow waiver. Gross household income applies. Table (updated 01/18/2024, for each additional family member add $6,725 to 125% and $10,760 to 200%):
| Size of Family Unit | 125% FPL (National) | 200% FPL (Max with Waiver) |
|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 1                  | $18,825             | $30,120                    |
| 2                  | $25,550             | $40,880                    |
| 3                  | $32,275             | $51,640                    |
| 4                  | $39,000             | $62,400                    |
eligibility.asset_limits: Assets are considered during screening (staff will ask about income and assets), but no specific dollar limits or exemptions detailed in guidelines.[1]
other_requirements: Arkansas resident
Civil legal matter only (no criminal cases like traffic tickets or fee-generating cases like personal injury)

Priority areas for seniors include: public benefits (Medicare, Medicaid, SSI), guardianships, powers of attorney, end-of-life instructions, evictions, landlord-tenant disputes, debt relief, consumer issues, employment rights, disability rights, domestic abuse, orders of protection.[2][3]

benefits.value: Free civil legal assistance including advice, representation, help with applications/appeals for benefits (e.g., Medicare, Medicaid, SSI), wills, powers of attorney, guardianships, evictions, debt collection defense, consumer scams, age discrimination, nursing home issues. No specific dollar amounts or hours stated; case-by-case based on priority and resources.[1][2][3]

methods: Online: Apply via link at https://arlegalaid.org/contact-us/apply-for-legal-aid.html (redirects to application portal)[9]
Phone: Helpline 1-800-9 LAW AID (1-800-952-9243); Tuesday evenings 5:15-7:15 PM for extended hours[1][9]
Note: No specific mail or in-person details listed; call helpline for guidance
application.processing_time: Staff try to take calls immediately; high volume may require callback. No specific processing timeline stated.[1]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; dependent on case volume and priority[1]
forms: Online application (no specific form name/number; accessed via arlegalaid.org apply link)
documents: Proof of income (to verify against guidelines)
Proof of assets
Details of legal issue (e.g., denial letters for benefits appeals, eviction notices, legal documents received)

geography.regional_variations: No regional differences specified; centralized helpline and online application serve statewide. Contact Area Agency on Aging for local elder assistance referrals if needed.[3]

gotchas: Criminal matters and fee-generating cases (e.g., personal injury) ineligible[2]
Income is gross household; waivers to 200% FPL only for specific meritorious factors, not automatic[1]
No age requirement—open to all qualifying low-income Arkansans, but seniors prioritized for relevant issues like benefits/guardianships[1][3]
Figures updated periodically (last 01/18/2024); verify current FPL via helpline as they adjust annually[1][6]
Groups/corporations rarely eligible unless all members qualify and lack private counsel means[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income guidelines use tiered FPL percentages with waiver option; no age or strict asset caps; civil-only with senior-relevant priorities; statewide via single provider with helpline/online focus

programs.name: Long-term Care Ombudsman

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; available to all residents of long-term care facilities regardless of financial status[5][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial tests apply[5][6]
other_requirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in Arkansas (e.g., nursing home, assisted living); anyone can contact on behalf of a resident, including family, friends, staff, or anonymously[5][6]

benefits.value: Advocacy services including investigating complaints, resolving issues related to resident rights, quality of life, Medicaid/Medicare, discharges/transfers, finances, restraints, guardianship/power of attorney, food quality, room temperature, social activities, care plans; education on rights; empowerment to speak for themselves; no specific dollar amounts or hours, provided as needed without reprisal[5][6]

methods: Phone: Contact local ombudsman (numbers posted in facilities; regional via Area Agency on Aging)[5]
In-person: Local ombudsman at long-term care facilities
Anyone can contact: friends, relatives, staff, anonymous callers[5][6]
application.processing_time: Immediate response to concerns; no formal processing timeline specified[5][6]
forms: No specific forms required for receiving services; complaints handled directly[5]
documents: No documents needed to request services; provide details of concern[5][6]

geography.restrictions: Limited to residents of long-term care facilities across Arkansas; access assured in each service area[4]
geography.regional_variations: Training varies slightly by region; delivered through local Area Agencies on Aging; different regional ombudsmen and offices[1][3]
offices_or_providers: Area Agencies on Aging (AAA) in each region; State Long-Term Care Ombudsman oversees[1][3][4]

gotchas: This is not a direct service or financial aid program but free advocacy for long-term care facility residents only—not for home care or non-residents; people often confuse it with becoming a volunteer ombudsman (which has training/age requirements) vs. receiving services (open to all facility residents); must contact local ombudsman posted in facilities, not a central application[1][5][6]
Volunteers/representatives have strict conflict of interest rules (e.g., no relation to facility staff/residents)[1][2][8]

programs.name: Family Caregiver Grant Program

other_requirements: Care recipient must reside in Arkansas and live independently or with family (not in a full-time care facility).[4]
Care recipient requires assistance of a caregiver for daily functions (must be stated in letter of diagnosis).[4]
Official diagnosis of dementia (any age) or chronic illness (age 60+ ) on physician’s official letterhead or prescription pad.[1][4]
If dementia, no age limit; otherwise, care recipient must be at least 60.[1]

benefits.value: $500 grant, usable for in-home respite care, adult day care, legal counseling, home care supplies, minor home modifications, mental health counseling, utilities, lawn maintenance, home cleaning, and more. Can apply every 6 months from prior approval date, as long as funds available.[4]

methods: Online or download at alzark.org/grants/[4]
Email: grants@alzark.org[4]
application.waitlist: Funds available as long as not depleted; no formal waitlist mentioned.[4]
forms: Family Assistance Program Grant application (available at alzark.org/grants/); must be fully completed and signed.[4]
documents: Letter of diagnosis of dementia (or chronic illness aged 60+) on official physician letterhead or prescription pad.[4]
Post-grant survey required, or lose future eligibility.[4]

geography.restrictions: Care recipient must reside in Arkansas.[4]
geography.regional_variations: Some related grants (e.g., via Alzheimer's Arkansas or Central Arkansas Area Agency on Aging) limited to specific counties like Pulaski, Saline, Monroe, Prairie, Lonoke, Faulkner, but core program statewide.[3][4]

gotchas: Must complete and return post-grant survey or lose eligibility for all future grants for both caregiver and care recipient.[4]
Care recipient cannot be in full-time care facility or receiving like-services from Medicaid, Medicare, AR Choices, etc. (for some sub-grants).[3][4]
Only twice per fiscal year (July 1-June 30), with 6-month wait between requests.[3][4]
Not for paid caregiver wages; financial grant for specific respite/supports only.[4]
